disease.name: Sigmoid Volvulus
disease.note: Twisting of sigmoid colon around its mesentery causing closed-loop obstruction. Common in elderly, chronic constipation, high-fibre diets. Endemic in East Africa.

history_questions.question: When did the abdominal distension start?
Is the distension progressive?
When did you last pass stool?
When did you last pass gas?
Is the pain colicky (comes and goes)?
Did the pain start suddenly or gradually?
Have you vomited?
Is the vomiting bilious (yellow/green)?
Have you had similar episodes before?
Do you have chronic constipation?
Do you use laxatives?
Have you lost weight recently?
Have you noticed blood in your stool?
Do you have a history of abdominal surgery?
What is your typical diet?
Do you have any neurological conditions (Parkinson's, stroke)?
Are you on any medications for nerves or pain?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries?
Do you have fever or chills?
Has the pain changed character (become constant)?
Do you feel short of breath?

historyFeatures.note: Late in sigmoid, early in cecal volvulus
May have history of recurrent volvulus
Common in elderly with redundant sigmoid

riskFactors.note: Dietary pattern risk in endemic regions

subtypes.name: Sigmoid Volvulus (Non-Ischemic)
subtypes.management: Endoscopic detorsion via flexible sigmoidoscopy. Insert rectal tube for decompression. Admit for observation. Elective sigmoid resection with primary anastomosis during same admission after bowel preparation.

subtypes.name: Sigmoid Volvulus with Ischemia/Gangrene
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy. Resection of gangrenous sigmoid colon. Hartmann procedure (end colostomy + rectal stump closure). Abdominal lavage. ICU admission. IV antibiotics. Delayed stoma reversal in 3-6 months.

subtypes.name: Cecal Volvulus
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy. Right hemicolectomy if non-viable. Cecopexy if viable. Endoscopic reduction rarely effective.

subtypes.name: Gastric Volvulus
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy. Gastric derotation. Gastropexy. Repair of diaphragmatic defect if present.

mimics.whyMimic: Can present with large bowel obstruction, distension, constipation
Can present with abdominal distension, vomiting, pain
Presents with massive colonic distension without mechanical obstruction
Elderly constipated patient with abdominal distension
LLQ pain, fever, altered bowel habit in elderly
Can cause bowel obstruction with distension

bedside.name: Abdominal X-Ray supine + erect
bedside.indication: Coffee bean sign, bent inner tube sign, air-fluid levels

bedside.name: Gastrografin enema
bedside.indication: Diagnostic and potentially therapeutic for sigmoid volvulus. Shows bird beak sign.

bedside.name: Digital Rectal Examination
bedside.indication: Assess rectal tone, exclude mass, check for blood

laboratory.name: WBC count
laboratory.indication: Leukocytosis suggests ischemia/strangulation. WBC >15 suggests gangrene.

laboratory.name: Lactate
laboratory.indication: Lactate >2 mmol/L suggests bowel ischemia. >4 suggests gangrene.

laboratory.name: Serum Amylase
laboratory.indication: Rule out pancreatitis if epigastric pain

imaging.name: Abdominal X-Ray (erect + supine)
imaging.indication: First-line. Coffee bean sign pathognomonic for sigmoid volvulus. Bent inner tube sign. Air-fluid levels.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: Gold standard. Shows mesenteric swirl sign, bird beak sign, transition point. Assesses bowel viability.

imaging.name: Gastrografin enema
imaging.indication: Bird beak stricture at rectosigmoid junction. Can be therapeutic.

management.protocolReference: WSES Guidelines for Acute Left-Sided Colonic Obstruction (2025)

resuscitation: IV fluids — Hartmann's solution or Normal Saline 1-2 L stat, then 125 mL/hr
Urinary catheter — monitor output, target >0.5 mL/kg/hr
NG tube — free drainage, gastric decompression
Type and crossmatch — 2 units PRBC on standby
analgesia: IV Morphine 2.5-5 mg titrated to pain (caution: may mask peritonitis)
Avoid NSAIDs if elderly or renal impairment
antibiotics: IV Ceftriaxone 2 g daily (or Cefuroxime 750 mg 8-hourly)
Duration: 5-7 days if gangrene, 24h if non-ischemic

non_ischemic.step1: Urgent flexible sigmoidoscopy + detorsion + rectal tube insertion (48-72 hours)
non_ischemic.step2: Decompression — leave rectal tube for 24-48 hours
non_ischemic.step4: Elective sigmoid resection with primary anastomosis (during same admission)

ischemic.step3: Assess bowel viability (color, peristalsis, pulsations, Wood's lamp fluorescein)
ischemic.step5: Mature proximal end as end colostomy (descending/sigmoid colostomy)
ischemic.step5_alt: If viable: sigmoid resection + primary anastomosis ± covering loop ileostomy

general: IV fluids until bowel function returns
NG tube on free drainage — remove when output <500 mL/day
Antibiotics for 5-7 days (if gangrene) or 24 hours
Early mobilization — out of bed day 1
Pain control — multimodal (paracetamol + NSAIDs + morphine PRN)
Wound care — observe for infection, keep dry for 48 hours
stoma_care: Mark stoma site pre-operatively if possible
Apply transparent stoma bag immediately post-op
Document stoma type (end colostomy), location, color (should be pink/red)
Monitor stoma hourly for first 24 hours: color, edema, output
Normal stoma output begins day 2-5: starts as mucus, then liquid stool
Stoma should be: pink/red, moist, slightly raised, non-tender
Emergency signs: dark/purple/black color = ischemia, excessive bleeding, retraction, parastomal hernia
Patient education: how to change bag, skin care, diet, when to seek help
Supply stoma starter pack at discharge
diet_advancement: Day 1-2: Sips of water if bowel sounds return
Day 2-3: Clear fluids (water, tea, clear soup)
Day 3-4: Soft diet (porridge, yoghurt, mashed potatoes, bananas)
Day 4-5: Full diet as tolerated — low residue initially
Avoid: beans, cabbage, nuts, seeds, maize in first 2 weeks
discharge_criteria: Passing stool via colostomy or per rectum
Stoma functioning and patient/carer confident with stoma care
Wound clean and dry, no signs of infection
Hb stable, lactate normal, WBC trending down

pathways.criteria: no peritonitis, no ischemia, sigmoid volvulus, lactate <2, no acidosis
pathways.treatment: Endoscopic detorsion + rectal tube. Elective sigmoid resection within same admission. Bowel prep and single-stage resection with anastomosis.

pathways.criteria: peritonitis, ischemia, gangrene, failed endoscopic reduction, lactate >2, acidosis, WBC >15
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy. Resection of non-viable bowel. Hartmann procedure for sigmoid (if unprepared). Primary anastomosis if viable and prepared. ICU care.

hartmann_procedure.title: Emergency Laparotomy + Sigmoid Resection + Hartmann's Procedure (End Colostomy)
hartmann_procedure.postOpDiagnosis: Gangrenous Sigmoid Volvulus / Ischemic Sigmoid Colon
hartmann_procedure.procedure: Emergency midline laparotomy, sigmoid colectomy, Hartmann's procedure, end colostomy formation
steps: Patient positioned supine, under general anaesthesia
Urinary catheter inserted
Midline laparotomy incision from xiphisternum to pubis
Entry into peritoneal cavity — note any free fluid, pus, or blood
Abdominal exploration — identify dilated sigmoid loop
Identify volvulus — note degree of twist (typically 180-720 degrees)
Assess bowel viability — colour, peristalsis, mesenteric pulsations
If non-viable: proceed with resection
Mobilize sigmoid colon — divide lateral peritoneal attachments
Identify left ureter — protect throughout
Identify proximal resection margin — descending colon, viable with good blood supply
Identify distal resection margin — rectosigmoid junction
Divide mesentery between clamps — ligate with 2-0 silk
Divide bowel at proximal and distal margins — using GIA stapler or between clamps
Send resected specimen for histology
Mature end colostomy: bring proximal descending colon through left rectus sheath at pre-marked site
Close rectal stump (Hartmann's pouch) with 3-0 PDS suture or stapler
Irrigate abdominal cavity with warm saline — at least 3 litres
Achieve haemostasis
Place pelvic drain if contaminated
Close abdomen in layers — PDS loop for rectus sheath, staples/sutures for skin
Apply transparent stoma bag
keyLandmarks: Left ureter — identified and protected throughout
Inferior mesenteric artery — ligated at origin if oncologic, otherwise preserve
Descending colon — proximal resection margin, viable with good pulsations
Pre-marked stoma site — through left rectus abdominis

elective_resection.title: Elective Sigmoid Colectomy with Primary Anastomosis
elective_resection.procedure: Laparoscopic/Open sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis
steps: Bowel preparation completed
Prophylactic antibiotics at induction
Laparoscopic approach: 4 ports (umbilical camera, right iliac, left flank, suprapubic)
Identify dilated/elongated sigmoid colon
Mobilize sigmoid by dividing lateral peritoneal reflection
Identify and protect left ureter
Divide mesentery with LigaSure/harmonic scalpel
Divide proximal margin (descending colon) and distal margin (upper rectum)
Extract specimen via Pfannenstiel or enlarged port site
Create end-to-end anastomosis using circular stapler (EEA 29-33 mm)
Test anastomosis — air leak test under saline
Close mesenteric defect
Close port sites

before_discharge: Stoma care teaching — patient and family must demonstrate changing bag independently
Dietician review — low residue diet teaching
Social work — home assessment if elderly living alone

after_discharge.action: Surgical clinic review — wound check, stoma assessment, histology results
Surgical clinic — assess for stoma reversal candidacy
If planned: colostomy reversal surgery (colonoscopy + water-soluble enema first)
Final follow-up after reversal — bowel function assessment
Colonoscopy surveillance (if history of diverticulitis or family history of CRC)

when_to_return: Vomiting and unable to tolerate fluids
Stoma becomes dark, purple, or black
Not opening bowels for >3 days (stoma or per rectum)

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Your large intestine (sigmoid colon) has twisted on itself like a knot. This blocks stool and gas from passing and can cut off blood supply to the bowel. We need to untwist it, either with a camera through the back passage or with an operation.
patient_education.hartmann_procedure_explanation: Because part of your bowel had lost its blood supply, we had to remove that section. The healthy end of the bowel has been brought out through your tummy wall as a stoma (colostomy). The remaining lower end has been stitched closed inside. This is a temporary situation — in 3-6 months, we can consider reversing the stoma and reconnecting the bowel.
patient_education.stoma_explanation: Your stoma is the end of your bowel coming through your tummy wall. It will pass stool into a bag that sticks to your skin around it. It has no nerve endings so it doesn't hurt. The stoma nurse will teach you how to care for it before you go home.
patient_education.diet_advice: Eat small, frequent meals. Chew food well. Avoid gas-forming foods (beans, cabbage, onions, carbonated drinks) in the first weeks. Drink plenty of water. Add new foods one at a time to see how your stoma responds.
patient_education.activity_advice: Avoid heavy lifting (>5 kg) for 6 weeks after surgery. Gentle walking is encouraged. You can shower with your stoma bag on. Avoid contact sports until cleared by your surgeon.

dischargeCriteria: Tolerating soft diet without vomiting or significant distension
Passing flatus or stool (via stoma or per rectum)
Pain controlled on oral analgesia (paracetamol, ibuprofen)
White cell count trending down or normal
Stoma: pink/red, functioning, patient/carer confident with care (if applicable)
Wound: clean, dry, no signs of infection
